queries.question: What are the roles at the table (players vs GM/referee/judge/MC), and what decisions does each role control?
queries.answer: Players decide what their characters do, describing their actions and choices. The Referee decides and describes the setting and what characters perceive, decides what happens as a result of the characters’ actions, creates and runs the adventure (including monsters), and decides the results of player decisions for their characters. The Referee does not tell players what their characters do; instead the Referee referees the results according to the rules and interprets what the rules mean. The rules are presented as guidelines rather than a complete procedure for every possibility: there is not a rule for everything, and when in doubt, the Referee makes a ruling.
queries.expected_answer_summary: Players choose and describe character actions; the Referee runs the world/adventure and adjudicates results, interpreting the rules and making rulings when needed.

gold_chunks.text: ![](images/0.jpg) — MONSTER DESCRIPTIONS — READING THE MONSTER DESCRIPTIONS — The following is a quick reference guide for how to read monster descriptions: — Hit Dice (HD) is the number of dice (d8) rolled to determine an individual creature’s hit points. If there is a plus or minus next to the number, you add or subtract that number from the total, only once. For example: for a creature with 4+1 hit dice, you would roll 4d8, and then add one more hit point. — Armor Class (AC) is explained in the rules for combat. If you are using the descending AC system (where a lower AC is better), disregard the numbers in brackets. If you are using the ascending system (where a high AC is better), use the numbers in brackets. — Attacks (Atk) includes the number of attacks the monster has, and the damage these attacks inflict if they hit. Monsters get a separate attack roll for each attack. Monster attack rolls are made on the Monster Attack Table (p. 38). Note: if you are using the “ascending AC system,” do not bother using the chart, because there is a quicker way: Roll to hit, add the monster’s hit dice, and if the result is equal to or greater than the character’s armor class, the attack hits. — Saving Throw (Save) means the target number (on a d20) the monster needs to meet or exceed in order to make a successful saving throw. — Morale (Morale) is an optional rule used to determine if monsters flee from combat or surrender. — Special (Special) is usually just a “flag” for the Referee, to remind him or her that the monster has some unusual ability or feature. If necessary, more explanation may be included in the monster description. — Move (Move) is the monster’s movement rate, and it is handled just as movement rates are handled for characters.

Percent in Lair (% in Lair) is used when a monster is encountered on a random table (such as the wilderness encounter tables). Roll 1d100, and if the result is equal to or lower than the number listed, the characters have stumbled upon the monster’s lair. For some monsters, the concept of a lair is largely meaningless (they don’t travel, don’t make a nest, or live on another plane of existence) and these might be either “0” or “100,” depending on what seems most appropriate. — Challenge Level (CL) is used to separate the monsters into “difficulty levels,” so when you are creating an adventure you have some guidelines about what the characters can handle and what they cannot. — XP (XP) tells how many experience points the adventuring party gains for killing the creature. In some cases, you may choose to award experience points for defeating a creature without killing it. Circumventing a monster by creative means, or capturing it to bring home, are two examples of when such an award might be made. — A short stat block after each monster description offers relevant details of the creature listed in a smaller form. It includes all of the information presented in the larger entry, including special attacks. These descriptions are often used in adventures to describe a monster quickly so you can keep playing without having to look them up.

![](images/0.jpg) — HOW TO PLAY — Once you have created a character, the Referee will describe the setting and what your character can see. The game might start in a rural peasant village, in a vast and teeming city spiked with towers and minarets, in a castle, in a tavern, or at the gates of an ancient tomb — that is up to the Referee. But from that point on, you describe what your character does. Going down stairs, attacking a dragon, talking to people on the street — all of these sorts of things are your decisions. The Referee tells you what happens as a result. Maybe those stairs lead down to a huge and echoing tomb, or perhaps that dragon attacks your character with a storm of fiery breath. These are the Referee’s decisions. The rules below are guidelines for how to handle certain events: combat, movement, healing, dying, and other important parts of the game. — Basically, you and the Referee work together, with the Referee handling the details of a dangerous fantasy world, and you deciding what your character does in it. The epic story of your character’s rise to greatness (or unfortunate death) is yours to create in the Referee’s world.

queries.question: When the outcome is uncertain, what is the default way to resolve it (dice/cards/other), and how do you read the result (target number, degrees of success, opposing rolls, etc.)?
queries.answer: The game resolves uncertainty using dice, with the specific die and success rule defined by the procedure you are using. The rules define standard polyhedral dice notation (d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, d20) and percentile dice (d100 as two d10s for tens and ones). For example, saving throws are resolved by rolling 1d20 and succeeding if the result is equal to or higher than the character’s saving throw target number (which depends on class/level, and monsters have a listed target number). In combat, initiative is resolved by each side rolling 1d6; the higher result wins initiative for that round. Surprise (when applicable) is checked by rolling 1d6 for a side: on a 1–2 the group is surprised (with stated class-based exceptions).
queries.expected_answer_summary: Uncertainty is resolved with dice; each procedure specifies what die to roll and how to read it (e.g., saves: d20 ≥ target; initiative: sides roll d6 high wins; surprise: d6 with 1–2 surprised).

gold_chunks.text: DICE — Swords & Wizardry uses several different kinds of dice, abbreviated according to how many sides they have. A four-sided die is called a d4, and if you roll 3 of them (adding the results together), that is written as 3d4. The six-sided die is a d6, the eight-sided die is a d8, the 10-sided die is a d10, the 12-sided die is a d12, and the 20-sided die is a d20. When the rules mention d100 (or percentile dice), roll two 10-sided dice, treating the first roll as the “tens” and the second roll as the “ones.” If you were to roll a 7 and then a 3, the result would be 73. A roll of 0 and 0 means a result of “100.”

SAVING THROWS — From time to time, a spell or some other kind of hazard requires you to make a “saving throw.” A successful saving throw means that the character (or monster) avoids the threat or lessens its effect. Each character class has a “Saving Throw” target number, which gets lower and lower — easier to beat, in other words — as a character gains levels. To make a saving throw, roll a d20. If the result is equal to or higher than the character’s saving throw target number, the saving throw succeeds.
Take note: Some character classes have better-than-average chances to make a saving throw against particular types of hazards. Magic-Users, for example, get a bonus of +2 to the die roll on their saving throws against spells of all kinds. These various bonuses are described in the explanation of each character class. Fighters, Paladins, and Rangers do not get these bonuses because their base saving throw numbers are better than the other classes across the board. — Monsters can also make saving throws; a monster’s saving throw target number is listed in its description.

INITIATIVE AND ORDER OF BATTLE — When the party of adventurers comes into contact with enemies, the order of events is as follows: — 1. Check for Surprise: The Referee determines if a surprise roll is required, and if so, both sides roll to see if they are surprised by the sudden encounter. See below for details of the surprise roll. — 2. Declare Spells: This is not an option for a group that was surprised; they have to wait until the beginning of the next round. See below for details of declaring spells. — 3. Determine Initiative: Each side rolls a d6 and the side with the highest result is the winner. One roll is made for each side, not for each individual. If this is the first round and one group was surprised, the other side gets to go first automatically, without having to roll a die. — 4. Movement and Missile Fire: Each combatant on the side that won initiative may move, or may fire any missile weapons. Then the losers of the initiative roll may move or fire missile weapons. — 5. Melee Combat and Spells: Each combatant on the side that won initiative makes any melee attacks (if in melee range) or casts spells. Spells take effect immediately. Spells cannot be cast if the caster is in melee combat with enemies. Losers of the initiative then make their attacks and cast spells. — 6. The round is complete. Go back to step 2 (Declare Spells) and repeat the process until the combat is finished.

queries.question: What named units of time does the game use during play (rounds, turns, scenes, phases), and how does each unit begin and end?
queries.answer: Time can advance in larger jumps when the Referee rules that hours or months pass, but the rules call out two key time units used for procedures: the turn and the combat round. A turn represents 10 minutes and is used to measure normal movement in dangerous places; a combat round represents 1 minute and is used to measure time while the party is in combat. The movement tables apply these units directly (walking/running speeds per turn, and combat movement per round). In combat, time advances round-by-round following an ordered sequence (check surprise if needed, declare spells, determine initiative, resolve movement/missile fire, resolve melee/spells, then the round ends and you repeat from declaring spells until combat ends). Special case: the Referee Guide describes a mass-combat procedure where combat rounds are longer (5 minutes for five-man units, 10 minutes for ten-man units), and initiative determines whether a side moves/attacks first or last within that larger-scale round.
queries.expected_answer_summary: Procedural time uses turns (10 minutes) and combat rounds (1 minute) for movement/combat; combat advances by repeating a step-by-step round sequence; mass combat can use longer 5–10 minute rounds (special rule).

gold_chunks.text: TIME — Sometimes the Referee will rule that “an hour passes,” or even, “a month passes,” in the life of the intrepid adventurers. However, two important time measurements need quick explanations. These are the “turn,” and the “combat round.” A turn represents 10 minutes, and a combat round is 1 minute. — Turns are used to measure normal movement when the adventurers are in a dangerous place; combat rounds are used to measure time when the party is in combat. See the Movement Rate tables as an example of how “rounds” and “turns” are used.
Indoor, Underground, and City Movement — A turn represents 10 minutes, and a round represents 1 minute of combat.

DescriptionSpeedResults
WalkingBase movement rate times 20 feet/turnMapping and careful observation of the surroundings are possible.
RunningBase movement rate times 40 feet/turnNo mapping permitted. Characters are automatically surprised and have no chance to surprise others. The sound of running may attract the attention of enemies.
CombatBase movement rate times 10 feet is how far the character can move in one round. Thus: Base movement of 6 = 60ft Base movement of 9 = 90ft Base movement of 12 = 120ft.Dashing around, battling your foes. Alternatively, running for your life. If some of the characters leave a combat (usually to circle around), they continue to move at the combat movement speed until they re-enter the combat area. This is actually faster than ordinary running speed, and incurs all the disadvantages of running.

Check for Surprise: The Referee determines if a surprise roll is required, and if so, both sides roll to see if they are surprised by the sudden encounter. See below for details of the surprise roll. — 2. Declare Spells: This is not an option for a group that was surprised; they have to wait until the beginning of the next round. See below for details of declaring spells. — 3. Determine Initiative: Each side rolls a d6 and the side with the highest result is the winner. One roll is made for each side, not for each individual. If this is the first round and one group was surprised, the other side gets to go first automatically, without having to roll a die. — 4. Movement and Missile Fire: Each combatant on the side that won initiative may move, or may fire any missile weapons. Then the losers of the initiative roll may move or fire missile weapons. — 5. Melee Combat and Spells: Each combatant on the side that won initiative makes any melee attacks (if in melee range) or casts spells. Spells take effect immediately. Spells cannot be cast if the caster is in melee combat with enemies. Losers of the initiative then make their attacks and cast spells. — 6. The round is complete. Go back to step 2 (Declare Spells) and repeat the process until the combat is finished.

INITIATIVE AND COMBAT ROUNDS — Combat rounds are five minutes long if troops are grouped into five-man units, and 10 minutes long if they are grouped into 10-man units. At the beginning of the battle, roll for initiative. Whichever side wins the initiative can choose to move first or last, and can also choose whether to attack first or last. For example, at the beginning of the Battle of Azure Wood, where the forces of Garfinkel the Wizard are opposing an invading goblin army, if Garfinkel wins the initiative he might choose to move first and attack first, to move first but attack last, to move and attack last, or to move last but attack first. All units on one side move or attack at the same time, and then those on the other side.

queries.question: What can a player character do when they act (actions, movement, reactions/interrupts, free actions), and what limits apply?
queries.answer: Combat actions are defined by the round procedure and its phases. In the standard combat sequence, each round follows an order of battle: check surprise (if needed), declare spells, roll initiative, then resolve movement and missile fire for the winning side and then the losing side, and finally resolve melee attacks and spellcasting for the winning side and then the losing side before completing the round and repeating. Within this structure, a combatant can take movement (including movement-like actions such as climbing onto tables or swinging from ropes) or fire missile weapons during the movement/missiles phase; and can make melee attacks (if in melee range) or cast spells during the melee/spells phase. Spellcasting has explicit limits: a spell must be declared before the initiative roll, and spells cannot be cast if the caster is in melee combat with enemies; if the enemy wins initiative and damages the spellcaster, the spell’s casting may be disturbed and the spell lost. The book also presents alternate combat sequences: Method 1 keeps a three-phase round where the initiative winner may move and attack or may hold initiative to act later (the initiative loser cannot hold); Method 2 resolves actions in dexterity order with spells first, then missiles, then melee, then movement; Method 3 uses a segmented round with action intervals modified by armor, spell level, dexterity, and other factors, allowing faster characters to act more often.
queries.expected_answer_summary: Combat options are phase-structured: move or fire missiles, then melee or cast spells, with limits like declaring spells before initiative and no spellcasting in melee; alternate sequences add holding initiative (Method 1), dex-order phases (Method 2), or segmented action timing (Method 3).

Surprise and Distance. The Referee determines if one side is surprised. As with all of the Referee’s decisions, this can be based on common sense instead of a die roll. It is possible, for example, that at the beginning of a combat round, Barzoom the Mystical (a character with dexterity 18) casts his spell first, followed by the enemy dragon (dexterity 17), followed by Simoon the Slow (a character with dexterity 16). queries.question: What information must be tracked to play correctly (character stats/resources/conditions, positioning, clocks/time), and where is it recorded (character sheet, tokens, track, notes)?
queries.answer: The rules call out that you complete a character sheet by recording your character’s statistics and equipment, and then play at the table with dice. Attribute scores (rolled 3d6 each) have mechanical effects that you should record, including bonuses/penalties (e.g., missile to-hit and Armor Class adjustments from Dexterity; Strength modifiers and carrying capacity/weight allowances; Constitution modifiers to hit points per hit die; Charisma limits on special hirelings and morale adjustments; and XP bonuses tied to Prime Attributes/Charisma/Wisdom). Treasure and money are tracked as gold pieces and other coinage, and treasure weight is tracked for encumbrance: weight is listed in pounds, a normal equipment load is assumed, and coins/gems have a defined weight; total carried weight determines base movement rate. Time is tracked in explicit procedural units (turns and combat rounds) that drive movement rates and other procedures. Consumable exploration resources like light sources have explicit durations (e.g., torch duration and lantern oil duration), which must be tracked to maintain light. Combat and exploration positioning/ranges are tracked at least in terms of distances/movement rates (feet per turn/round underground; yards per round outdoors), and combat procedures rely on tracked values like Armor Class and hit points.
queries.expected_answer_summary: Track character sheet stats and equipment; attribute-derived bonuses; treasure/coin and its weight for encumbrance and movement; procedural time units (turn/round); consumables like light duration; and combat-relevant values like AC/HP and distances/movement.

gold_chunks.text: COMMON TERMS — You will discover several common terms (and their abbreviations) while reading. Swords & Wizardry itself is a role-playing game, shortened to RPG. The Referee may also be known as the Game Master, or GM. As a player, your character will be a Player Character (PC), while the Referee will provide Non-Player Characters (NPCs). “Experience Points” (XP) are the measure of a character’s growing expertise and ability. “Hit Dice” (HD) are the number of dice a character or monster rolls to determine total “Hit Points” (hp), the amount of damage one can handle before becoming incapacitated. “Armor Class” (AC) is a measure of protection against attack. A “Saving Throw” represents the attempt to avoid or mitigate some type of unusual attack. Gold pieces (often just called gp) are the most important medium of exchange. Do not worry about trying to memorize all this; most of it will be evident as you read through.

![](images/0.jpg) — CREATING A CHARACTER — Your first step as a player is to create a character. A Referee who is just learning the rules should also become familiar with how this part of the game works.

GETTING STARTED — Most likely, you already know basically how this game works. One of you is the “Referee,” who runs the game world: The Referee designs the adventures, makes decisions and rolls dice for the monsters, describes what the characters see, and judges the effects of their actions. The rest of you are the “players,” each of you taking on the role of a “player character” who might be a powerful wizard, a hard-bitten warrior, or any other sort of fantasy avatar representing you in the game world. You do not need to use miniatures, although they can sometimes help clarify who was standing underneath the stone block when it fell, and other such important questions. — The first step is to complete a character sheet, recording your character’s statistics and equipment; the next step is to sit down around a table with some dice and start playing! Unless, of course, you are the Referee, in which case you will have to prepare the adventure first. You have the Referee Book to help you with your role!

queries.question: How does initiative work and what is the basic combat sequence for resolving a round?
queries.answer: Each combat round follows a fixed 6-step sequence: (1) Check for Surprise — the Referee determines whether a surprise roll is needed; each side rolls 1d6 and is surprised on a 1 or 2 (Monk/Ranger parties are surprised only on a 1); one roll per side, not per individual. (2) Declare Spells — any player intending to cast must declare before the initiative roll; a surprised side skips this step and waits until the next round. (3) Determine Initiative — each side rolls 1d6; highest result wins; on a tie the Referee may treat actions as simultaneous or have both sides re-roll. (4) Movement and Missile Fire — initiative winners move or fire first; then losers do the same; winners cannot hold to see what foes do. (5) Melee Combat and Spells — initiative winners attack in melee and cast spells first; losers follow; creatures killed by winners are already dead and cannot act; Turning Undead also occurs here. (6) Round Complete — check ongoing effects, then return to step 2 and repeat until combat ends (one side flees, surrenders, or is entirely slain). Spells declared in step 2 may be lost if the caster takes damage before the melee/spells phase resolves. The book also presents alternate combat sequences (Method No. 1–3) that change the initiative/action ordering (including holding initiative, dexterity-ordered casting/attacks, and segmented action intervals), but the standard 6-step order above is the basic procedure.
queries.expected_answer_summary: 6-step round: Surprise check, Declare Spells, Roll initiative (d6 per side, high wins), Movement/Missiles (winners first), Melee/Spells (winners first), then repeat from step 2.

1. SURPRISE — In general, a group cannot be surprised if they are already aware of an opponent’s presence even though they have not yet seen them — due to hearing them on the other side of a door, for instance. — If one side is surprised, the other side automatically gets to take actions before the first initiative roll. If they also win that initiative roll, this means they will have taken two actions before the other side has a chance to do anything. If surprise is a possibility, roll 1d6 for either or both groups, as appropriate. (Roll only once for each side, not for each individual character or monster.) If the result is a 1 or a 2, the group is surprised. A party that includes a Monk or Ranger, however, is surprised only on a roll of 1. It is possible for both groups to be surprised, effectively canceling out the effect on both sides. — And here is something really nasty: If a group is surprised, each person (or creature) in it has a 1-in-4 chance to drop one or more handheld items! — Underground or in a dungeon, the distance between two groups when they discover each other is 1d6 x 10 feet. That is very close; the monsters may already be inside the radius of the adventurers' light source. Outside, the distance will be about 10 times this number, or, if the visibility is bad, three times. Anything affecting visibility could also affect surprise distance. For instance, someone with darkvision will be able to see up to 60 feet clearly at night or underground. A party carrying a light will be visible from much farther away, raising the possibility of an ambush. Other factors that may affect surprise are related to terrain types — thick woods or crowded city streets may decrease visibility and make it easier to surprise travelers. These and similar factors are left up to the judgment of the Referee.

Take note: Monsters do not always leap to the attack. Unless they are on the alert for intruders, the Referee may roll 2d6 to determine whether the monsters attack if they surprise the adventurers. On a roll of 2–6, the monsters are hostile and attack; on a roll of 7–9, the monsters withhold judgment and wait to see if the adventurers are hostile; on a roll of 10–12, the monsters have a positive initial reaction to seeing the adventurers and might even be convinced to offer some help — if they are well paid, of course!

5. Combat. Melee (hand-to-hand) combat is resolved, with each opponent making attacks in order of dexterity. See “The Attack Roll” below.

queries.question: How does a character regain the ability to use their most powerful abilities (spell slots, power points, charges, or other expendable resources), and what restrictions apply?
queries.answer: Magic-Users prepare spells by reading from their spellbook and forcing selected spell formulae into their minds, preparing as many spells as they can mentally sustain; a spell can be prepared multiple times using available memory slots. Once a prepared spell is cast, it disappears from the Magic-User’s ability to cast until it is prepared again. The text does not state a fixed daily cadence for Magic-User preparation (unlike Clerics and Druids, who explicitly select and pray for a set of spells each day). If a spellcaster suffers damage while casting, the spell is lost; in the standard combat procedure, spells are declared before the initiative roll and casting starts at the beginning of the round, so if the enemy wins initiative and damages the spellcaster, the casting may be disturbed and the spell lost.
queries.expected_answer_summary: Magic-Users prepare from a spellbook into memory slots (can prepare a spell multiple times); after casting it must be prepared again. If damaged while casting, the spell is lost; in combat, declaring happens before initiative and damage before resolution can lose the spell.

gold_chunks.text: SPELLS — Casting a spell starts at the beginning of the combat round. Spells cannot be cast if the caster is in melee combat, although the Referee might rule that even if the caster is within 10 feet of an opponent, if blows have not been traded, then a spell can be cast. Wands and staffs are not subject to the restriction at all; they can cast their magic during melee combat. If the spellcaster suffers damage while casting a spell, the spell is lost. When the spell actually takes effect depends on which Combat Sequence Method is being used. — ![](images/0.jpg)

2. DECLARE SPELLS — Any player whose character is going to cast a spell must say so before the initiative roll. (The Referee makes a similar determination for the opponents.) The casting of any spell starts at the beginning of the combat round. Thus, if the enemies win the initiative roll and damage the spellcaster, the spell’s casting may be disturbed and the spell lost. (This is the Swords & Wizardry interpretation of several places in the Original Rules where a spell must be “ready.”)

queries.question: What happens at 0 hit points, and when does a character die from damage?
queries.answer: When damage reduces a character (or creature) to 0 hit points, the character is unconscious. If hit points are brought down to –1 or lower, the character dies. The book also describes a potential house rule: a character remains alive (bleeding at 1 hp/round without assistance) until reaching negative hit points equal to the character’s level (e.g., a 5th-level character dies at –5). For healing, natural recovery is 1 hit point per 2 days of uninterrupted rest (many Referees accelerate this to 1/day), and four weeks of rest returns a character to full hit points regardless of how many were lost.
queries.expected_answer_summary: 0 HP = unconscious; –1 HP or lower = dead. Natural healing is 1 HP per 2 days of uninterrupted rest; four weeks restores to full.

gold_chunks.text: DAMAGE AND DEATH — When a character (or creature) is hit, the amount of damage is deducted from hit points. When total hit points reach 0, the character is unconscious, and if hit points are brought down to -1 or lower, the character dies. — A good potential house rule is attributed to Gary Gygax’s gaming table, a rumor that might or might not be true. It allows a character to remain alive (although bleeding to death at the rate of 1 hp/round if no assistance is rendered) until the character reaches negative hit points equal to the character’s level. In other words, a 5th-level character actually dies only upon reaching -5 hit points.

queries.question: When NPCs or enemies encounter the player characters for the first time, or when combat turns against them, how does the system determine their attitude or willingness to continue fighting?
queries.answer: Reaction: monsters do not always attack on sight. In particular, if monsters surprise the adventurers and are not on the alert for intruders, the Referee may roll 2d6 to determine their initial reaction: 2–6 = hostile and attack; 7–9 = withhold judgment and wait to see if the adventurers are hostile; 10–12 = positive initial reaction and may even offer help (possibly for pay). Morale: most monsters will not fight a hopeless battle; they may retreat, flee, or surrender. The Referee decides when monsters abandon the battle based on the situation and the monsters’ intelligence, and NPC allies may also flee if survival looks grim. The book points to an optional morale system elsewhere.
queries.expected_answer_summary: Reaction can be a 2d6 roll (2–6 hostile attack; 7–9 wait; 10–12 positive) in the surprise/alertness context. Morale is primarily Referee-adjudicated unless using the optional morale system.

gold_chunks.text: 1. SURPRISE — In general, a group cannot be surprised if they are already aware of an opponent’s presence even though they have not yet seen them — due to hearing them on the other side of a door, for instance. — If one side is surprised, the other side automatically gets to take actions before the first initiative roll. If they also win that initiative roll, this means they will have taken two actions before the other side has a chance to do anything. If surprise is a possibility, roll 1d6 for either or both groups, as appropriate. (Roll only once for each side, not for each individual character or monster.) If the result is a 1 or a 2, the group is surprised. A party that includes a Monk or Ranger, however, is surprised only on a roll of 1. It is possible for both groups to be surprised, effectively canceling out the effect on both sides. — And here is something really nasty: If a group is surprised, each person (or creature) in it has a 1-in-4 chance to drop one or more handheld items! — Underground or in a dungeon, the distance between two groups when they discover each other is 1d6 x 10 feet. That is very close; the monsters may already be inside the radius of the adventurers' light source. Outside, the distance will be about 10 times this number, or, if the visibility is bad, three times. Anything affecting visibility could also affect surprise distance. For instance, someone with darkvision will be able to see up to 60 feet clearly at night or underground. A party carrying a light will be visible from much farther away, raising the possibility of an ambush. Other factors that may affect surprise are related to terrain types — thick woods or crowded city streets may decrease visibility and make it easier to surprise travelers. These and similar factors are left up to the judgment of the Referee.

Take note: Monsters do not always leap to the attack. Unless they are on the alert for intruders, the Referee may roll 2d6 to determine whether the monsters attack if they surprise the adventurers. On a roll of 2–6, the monsters are hostile and attack; on a roll of 7–9, the monsters withhold judgment and wait to see if the adventurers are hostile; on a roll of 10–12, the monsters have a positive initial reaction to seeing the adventurers and might even be convinced to offer some help — if they are well paid, of course!

MORALE — Certain monsters, such as mindless or undead creatures, are fearless and always fight to the death. The majority, however, will not continue to fight a hopeless battle; they will seek to retreat, flee, or surrender. The Referee decides when monsters abandon the battle and retreat, based on the situation and the monsters' intelligence. Keep in mind that the party’s own non-player character allies might decide to flee if their prospects of survival look grim. See page 39 for an optional system to handle morale. — ![](images/0.jpg)

queries.question: How does encumbrance affect movement and what needs to be tracked to apply it?
queries.answer: Encumbrance is tracked as carried weight (in pounds), including gear and treasure. A normal load of miscellaneous equipment (excluding armor and weapons) is assumed to be 10 pounds, and treasure adds weight: each coin and gem weighs one-tenth of a pound. Total carried weight determines base movement rate: up to 75 lb = 12; 76–100 lb = 9; 101–150 lb = 6; 151–300 lb (300-lb max) = 3. Any Strength-based Carry Modifier is added to the total for determining which bracket you fall into (e.g., +10 increases the threshold before dropping a movement category; –10 reduces it).
queries.expected_answer_summary: Track carried pounds (gear + treasure; coin/gem = 0.1 lb each). Movement brackets: ≤75→12, 76–100→9, 101–150→6, 151–300→3 (300 max), adjusted by Strength Carry Modifier.

gold_chunks.text: ![](images/0.jpg) — WEIGHT AND MOVEMENT — Weight is listed in pounds. A “normal” level of miscellaneous equipment (not including armor and weapons) is assumed to weigh 10 pounds. Treasure is added to this, with each coin and gem weighing one-tenth of a pound. These are big, heavy coins and gems, but that is just the way of things in a fantasy world. Coins should clink, and gems should be the size of dice. — All characters, depending on the weight of the armor and equipment they are carrying, have a base movement rate as follows:

![](images/0.jpg) — MONSTER DESCRIPTIONS — READING THE MONSTER DESCRIPTIONS — The following is a quick reference guide for how to read monster descriptions: — Hit Dice (HD) is the number of dice (d8) rolled to determine an individual creature’s hit points. If there is a plus or minus next to the number, you add or subtract that number from the total, only once. For example: for a creature with 4+1 hit dice, you would roll 4d8, and then add one more hit point. — Armor Class (AC) is explained in the rules for combat. If you are using the descending AC system (where a lower AC is better), disregard the numbers in brackets. If you are using the ascending system (where a high AC is better), use the numbers in brackets. — Attacks (Atk) includes the number of attacks the monster has, and the damage these attacks inflict if they hit. Monsters get a separate attack roll for each attack. Monster attack rolls are made on the Monster Attack Table (p. 38). Note: if you are using the “ascending AC system,” do not bother using the chart, because there is a quicker way: Roll to hit, add the monster’s hit dice, and if the result is equal to or greater than the character’s armor class, the attack hits. — Saving Throw (Save) means the target number (on a d20) the monster needs to meet or exceed in order to make a successful saving throw. — Morale (Morale) is an optional rule used to determine if monsters flee from combat or surrender. — Special (Special) is usually just a “flag” for the Referee, to remind him or her that the monster has some unusual ability or feature. If necessary, more explanation may be included in the monster description. — Move (Move) is the monster’s movement rate, and it is handled just as movement rates are handled for characters.

Percent in Lair (% in Lair) is used when a monster is encountered on a random table (such as the wilderness encounter tables). Roll 1d100, and if the result is equal to or lower than the number listed, the characters have stumbled upon the monster’s lair. For some monsters, the concept of a lair is largely meaningless (they don’t travel, don’t make a nest, or live on another plane of existence) and these might be either “0” or “100,” depending on what seems most appropriate. — Challenge Level (CL) is used to separate the monsters into “difficulty levels,” so when you are creating an adventure you have some guidelines about what the characters can handle and what they cannot. — XP (XP) tells how many experience points the adventuring party gains for killing the creature. In some cases, you may choose to award experience points for defeating a creature without killing it. Circumventing a monster by creative means, or capturing it to bring home, are two examples of when such an award might be made. — A short stat block after each monster description offers relevant details of the creature listed in a smaller form. It includes all of the information presented in the larger entry, including special attacks. These descriptions are often used in adventures to describe a monster quickly so you can keep playing without having to look them up.

queries.question: Outside of structured combat, how does the game track the passage of time during exploration or travel, and what time-based events can interrupt a group's progress?
How does surprise work and what advantage does the surprising side get?
queries.answer: If surprise is a possibility, roll 1d6 once per side (not per individual). A side is surprised on a 1–2; a party that includes a Monk or Ranger is surprised only on a 1. It is possible for both groups to be surprised, canceling out the effect. A group generally cannot be surprised if it is already aware of an opponent’s presence. If one side is surprised, the other side automatically gets to take actions before the first initiative roll; if that side also wins the first initiative roll, it can take two actions before the surprised side can do anything. Additionally, if a group is surprised, each person (or creature) in it has a 1-in-4 chance to drop one or more handheld items.
queries.expected_answer_summary: Surprise is a 1d6 roll per side (1–2 surprised; Monk/Ranger party only on 1); surprised side grants opponents actions before initiative (potentially two actions before it can act).

queries.question: How do characters advance over time, and what changes when they do (level, skills, attributes, abilities, or equivalent progression)?

required_gold_rationale.5addf70c6497254c723d72229c681e36debc3b89: Provides the core progression loop: characters gain levels through advancement and, as they do, get more hit points, better equipment, magic items, and learn spells. This is the clearest general statement of how advancement changes characters over time.
required_gold_rationale.263a8e8f1e95ccb8622b693d029668beb60ba864: Supplies a concrete advancement table showing the standard structure of progression: XP thresholds lead to higher levels, increased hit dice/hit points, and improved saving throws, with continued per-level gains after name level. This operationalizes the 'level/skills/attributes/abilities or equivalent progression' part.

gold_chunks.text: GAINING EXPERIENCE — Characters are awarded experience points (XP) for killing monsters and accumulating treasure. Monsters each have a set experience point value (given in the monster’s statistics), and each gold piece acquired earns one point as well. It may seem odd to award experience for treasure, but keep in mind that Swords & Wizardry is not just about slaying monsters — it is about outwitting your foes when you can! In fact, sometimes trickery and guile are the only way to handle a powerful monster such as a dragon or a demon. Skilled players avoid risking their characters’ lives if there is another, smarter way to walk out of the dungeon with a backpack full of gems and loot. — XP Bonus: Each Character Class has one or more Prime Attributes listed in its class description. If a character has this Prime Attribute at 13 or higher, all experience point awards to the character are increased by 5%. (If the class has more than one Prime Attribute, all must be 13 or above to earn to bonus.) Additionally, if the character has a Charisma score of 13 or more, experience point awards are increased by 5%. Finally, if the character has a Wisdom score of 13+, the character gains a third 5% bonus. Thus high Wisdom or Charisma may earn bonuses twice for a character, as the Prime Attribute as well as individually. It is possible for a character to gain as many as three 5% bonuses to experience point awards for a total increase of 15%. — Some multi-classed characters do not receive this bonus; see the rules for multi-classed characters for details. — When your character accumulates enough experience points to reach a new level, you roll another hit die and add that number to the character’s hit points. He or she may gain new spells or other abilities, as applicable, and combat skills may also increase. In other words, your character has become more powerful and can now pursue greater challenges!

LEVEL ADVANCEMENT — The abilities of a class are gained as soon as a new level is achieved in that class, but a new hit die is not gained until the character has advanced a level in all classes. In other words, a dwarfven Fighter/Thief would not gain a “Thief” hit die at level 1/2; instead, the character would receive a second multi-class hit die (the averaged result of the roll of a d8 and a d4) after reaching 2nd level as a Fighter also. — If a multi-classed character reaches the point where one class is “capped,” and the character cannot advance in that class any more, the character still gains only a new hit die when both (or all three) levels would have been reached. For example, an elven Fighter/Magic-User reaches 4th-level Fighter and 4th-level Magic-User with 10,000 XP in each class, when the Magic-User experience reaches 4th level. Now the player rolls a new hit die, rolling a d8 and a d4 and taking the average of the result. When the character later reaches 16,000 XP in each class, normally that would mean gaining a level as a Fighter. However, since an Elf is capped at 4th level as a Fighter, there is no result. When the Elf reaches 20,000 XP in each class and becomes a 5th-level magic-user, the new hit die is rolled. (It is still an average of rolling a d8 and a d4.) For the sake of example, if somehow the Elf had lost XP in the Fighter class, reaching 10,000 XP in the Magic-User class (4th level) without first reaching 8,000 XP (4th level) as a Fighter, then the Elf would not gain an additional hit die until reaching 8,000 XP as a Fighter. This situation is not likely to arise.

Note that non-humans cannot be Assassins, Druids, Monks, Paladins, or Rangers as player characters. — Continued Advancement — Non-human characters are limited in their advancement in many classes. If a character has only a single class, rather than completely halting the advancement in that class, the Referee might allow the character to continue gaining levels more slowly. A severe penalty would be applied to the amount of experience being gained once the ordinary maximum (as indicated in the appropriate ancestry description) is attained. The penalty might, for example, be 50%, although any XP Bonus would still apply. — If a character with multiple classes has maximum limits to levels in all classes, the Referee might allow the character to advance slowly in one class after all limits have been reached. When the multi-classed character has reached the highest permitted level in all classes, the Referee may let the player choose one class in which the character can continue to advance. The character could then continue gaining levels, but with a severe penalty to the amount of experience being gained, such as the 50% suggested above. In addition, the usual procedure of dividing experience points among all classes still applies.

Strength Bonuses: Unlike most other character classes, Fighters with a high Strength can have bonuses to hit and on damage. — Saving Throw Bonus: Fighters have a +1 bonus on all saving throws except against spells (including those from wands and staffs). — Establish Stronghold (9th): At 9th level, a Fighter may establish a stronghold and attract a body of men-at-arms who swear their loyalty to the character as their liege lord (or lady). Most likely, the castle attracts peasants seeking safe places to establish homes, and the Fighter becomes more powerful and influential as the number of such followers increases. The stronghold will likely be a small territory carved from the wilderness, held as a freehold by the self-enmobled character.

queries.question: What are the minimum required steps and choices to create a playable character?

required_gold_rationale.a4b99bf4548087acf3cb9c7d4378583d5a3685f2: Establishes the first concrete required character-creation step: roll 3d6 for each of the six attributes.
required_gold_rationale.ac54e0be6924789e71eab0190e1a7035d35fe982: Establishes that choosing a character class is a required choice after rolling attributes, and notes interaction with later ancestry/alignment choices.

gold_chunks.text: ![](images/0.jpg) — ROLL ATTRIBUTE SCORES — The basic attributes of a character are numbers representing Strength (muscle power), Dexterity (quickness and coordination), Constitution (general health and hardiness), Intelligence (education and IQ), Wisdom (common sense), and Charisma (leadership). The first step to creating your character is to roll 3d6 for each of the six attribute scores. If this is a long-term game and you want to play a specific character class, the Referee will probably allow you to shift the scores around — in some fashion — if your dice rolls are a disaster for that particular kind of character. Keep in mind that your character will almost certainly have at least one score that is lower than average. This is not a game of armored superheroes! The great wizards and knights of the world do not begin as superior specimens of human perfection; they are flawed like anyone else. What truly makes heroes of legend, in the end, is not what they were born with; it is what they achieve, in a risky life full of adventure, peril, and courage — and sometimes lots of trickery and guile, too.

CHOOSE A CHARACTER CLASS — Once you roll up your attribute scores, the next step is to choose a “character class.” The available classes are: Assassin, Cleric, Druid, Fighter, Magic-User, Monk, Paladin, Ranger, and Thief. Your Referee may also have invented other character classes that are available to you. Decide what sort of character you want to play, keeping in mind that certain attribute scores work well with certain character classes. Later you will choose your character’s ancestry and “Alignment,” which is the character’s alliance with Law or Chaos. Some classes can be of any ancestry and alignment, but others have restrictions, so you should be aware of this when you are considering your choice.

CHOOSE A CHARACTER ANCESTRY — In a fantasy world, humans often are not alone. Elves may populate the great forests, Dwarves carve their halls and mines into the heart of the earth, and Hallfings reside in the comfortable hill-houses of their bucolic shires. The Referee will inform you if there are other types of non-humans available for use as player characters.

GETTING STARTED — Most likely, you already know basically how this game works. One of you is the “Referee,” who runs the game world: The Referee designs the adventures, makes decisions and rolls dice for the monsters, describes what the characters see, and judges the effects of their actions. The rest of you are the “players,” each of you taking on the role of a “player character” who might be a powerful wizard, a hard-bitten warrior, or any other sort of fantasy avatar representing you in the game world. You do not need to use miniatures, although they can sometimes help clarify who was standing underneath the stone block when it fell, and other such important questions. — The first step is to complete a character sheet, recording your character’s statistics and equipment; the next step is to sit down around a table with some dice and start playing! Unless, of course, you are the Referee, in which case you will have to prepare the adventure first. You have the Referee Book to help you with your role!

SETTING UP YOUR CHARACTER SHEET — Information about your character goes on a character sheet, which can be a simple sheet of paper or you can use a preprinted form. — The blank character sheet provided in this book may be photocopied or otherwise reproduced for personal use, if desired. — ![](images/1.jpg) — IDENTITY — ATTRIBUTES — EQUIPMENT,

queries.question: How does the game set task difficulty and apply situational modifiers (bonuses/penalties, advantage/disadvantage, edge, dice added/removed)?

required_gold_rationale.dbca21a0e7994bb9ecd3b237ab4e2139b32b6b2c: Directly states how the Referee applies situational modifiers: assigns bonuses and penalties for terrain/features, usually small (+/-1 or 2) with larger cases like -4 for extreme cover. This is the clearest anchor for situational modifiers.
required_gold_rationale.934ce5b65c11f2bcefbe8f1b916d5f4acdb051ea: Establishes the core mechanic that attack rolls add bonuses and subtract penalties, with examples such as cover and cursed weapons. This grounds how modifiers are operationally applied in play.

gold_chunks.text: TERRAIN FEATURES — Characters and monsters will hide behind things, stand on things, lie prone, fight from higher ground, shoot arrows from within concealing mists or tree branches, and take every possible advantage of the combat terrain. The Referee will assign bonuses and penalties for terrain features. Most such bonuses will only be  \( \pm1 \)  or 2, depending on the degree of cover or the benefit of higher ground. Trying to hit someone through an arrow slit in a castle wall might have a penalty of  \( -4 \)  (as suggested in the Holmes Basic Set in 1977). Remember, however, that the penalty to hit an invisible opponent is only  \( -4 \) , so  \( \pm1 \)  is about the outside limit for terrain adjustments on the to-hit roll.

THE ATTACK ROLL — The most important rule to understand about combat is the attack roll. To have a character attack with a weapon, a player rolls a d20 and adds any bonuses to the result. These “to-hit” bonuses may include a Fighter’s strength bonus, a dexterity bonus (for attacks with missile weapons), and any bonuses for magic weapons. The player or the Referee also subtracts any “to-hit” penalties, which might come from using cursed weapons, enemies hiding behind cover, etc.). The total attack roll is then compared to a table to see if the attack hits. If the attack roll is equal to or higher than the number shown on the table, the attack succeeds. — If an attack hits, it inflicts damage, a number of hit points determined by the type of weapon the attacker is using. Damage is subtracted from the defender’s hit point total. (See “Damage and Death”).

A unit fighting from the higher ground has a choice to make each round; it can gain a +4 on its attack roll or it can force all attackers to take a -4 on attack rolls against the unit during that round. — Units fighting inside a forest are immune to missile fire unless they are lined up at the edge, using trees as cover, in which case all enemy attacks are made at -4. This includes melee attacks; holding the edge of a tree line is an advantage even in close combat. — Defending from behind a wall causes enemy attacks to be made at -4. (Note: Defenders behind a wall at the edge of a tree line do not get modifiers from both the wall and the trees; only one of these modifiers is applied to enemy attacks.) Thus, if a unit is on top of a castle wall, defending against archers firing from below, an attack against them might be made at -8 (-4 for the higher ground and -4 for the wall). — If a unit is flanking another unit (attacking from the side), it gains +4 to hit. A unit attacking another unit from the rear attacks at +4 and also inflicts double normal damage. — Movement Rule: A unit cannot turn and move in the same round unless it is a mounted cavalry unit. — These rules should be enough to handle most situations that arise, short of the characters becoming involved in a full-scale war. Keep in mind also that these are not “official” rules, just a quick outline of one way to play out the sort of battles in which the characters might find themselves as commanders or participants. The players and Referee are completely free to use another set of rules to suit their purposes.

MODIFIERS — Large-scale combat depends greatly upon the terrain, and gaining superior terrain is an important part of such combats. Fighting down a slope or from higher ground is a major factor, perhaps the most important one. Do not forget, though: Even if a unit cannot be hit, when an aggressor rolls a natural 20, the attack still inflicts one-quarter of its normal damage.

queries.question: When a character is hit, how is damage applied and translated into mechanical harm before the dying/death stage?

required_gold_rationale.884ce2952bfaf3c2c0e078ad002ac5ee3ec5f6e0: Directly states how damage is applied mechanically after a hit: damage is deducted from hit points, with the thresholds at 0 hp (unconscious) and -1 or lower (dead). This is the core rule the question asks about before/into the dying-death boundary.
required_gold_rationale.934ce5b65c11f2bcefbe8f1b916d5f4acdb051ea: Provides the immediate bridge from being hit to harm: if an attack hits, it inflicts damage determined by the weapon, and that damage is subtracted from the defender’s hit point total. This operationalizes how a hit becomes mechanical harm.

gold_chunks.text: DAMAGE AND DEATH — When a character (or creature) is hit, the amount of damage is deducted from hit points. When total hit points reach 0, the character is unconscious, and if hit points are brought down to -1 or lower, the character dies. — A good potential house rule is attributed to Gary Gygax’s gaming table, a rumor that might or might not be true. It allows a character to remain alive (although bleeding to death at the rate of 1 hp/round if no assistance is rendered) until the character reaches negative hit points equal to the character’s level. In other words, a 5th-level character actually dies only upon reaching -5 hit points.

THE ATTACK ROLL — The most important rule to understand about combat is the attack roll. To have a character attack with a weapon, a player rolls a d20 and adds any bonuses to the result. These “to-hit” bonuses may include a Fighter’s strength bonus, a dexterity bonus (for attacks with missile weapons), and any bonuses for magic weapons. The player or the Referee also subtracts any “to-hit” penalties, which might come from using cursed weapons, enemies hiding behind cover, etc.). The total attack roll is then compared to a table to see if the attack hits. If the attack roll is equal to or higher than the number shown on the table, the attack succeeds. — If an attack hits, it inflicts damage, a number of hit points determined by the type of weapon the attacker is using. Damage is subtracted from the defender’s hit point total. (See “Damage and Death”).

queries.question: How do characters recover lost health or remove injuries outside the dying/death state, and what limits apply?

required_gold_rationale.d1ca3aa5782fec617d0f41ffc4287939e9a00ad4: This is the core rule for recovering lost health outside dying/death: natural healing occurs at 1 hp per 2 days of uninterrupted rest, with a common referee acceleration to 1 hp/day, and 4 weeks of rest restoring full hit points regardless of losses. It directly answers both the recovery method and its limits.

gold_chunks.text: HEALING — In addition to the various magical means of restoring hit points, a character recovers naturally at the rate of 1 hit point per 2 days of uninterrupted rest (many Referees accelerate this to 1 hit point per day). Four weeks of rest return a character to full hit points regardless of how many hit points may have been lost.

queries.question: What procedure, if any, resolves uncertain social outcomes (persuasion, intimidation, negotiation), and what inputs modify it?

required_gold_rationale.fe4e77aeaec82833fdc3870e2e3729f8089e6a0a: Directly answers the question: uncertain social outcomes like bargaining, persuasion, and getting out of trouble are explicitly not resolved with dice rolls; instead, player skill determines outcomes, though dice may be used only for a monster’s initial reaction before negotiations begin.

gold_chunks.text: NEGOTIATION AND DIPLOMACY — Some combats can be averted with a few well-chosen words, which may include lies. If a party is outmatched or the monsters do not seem to be carrying much in the way of loot, the party might elect to brazen their way through, in an attempt to avoid combat — or at least delay it until conditions become more favorable. Striking bargains, persuading monsters or non-player characters to do things, and getting out of trouble by using wits, are all important parts of the game. Do not replace them with dice rolls! Using dice to determine a monster’s initial reaction before negotiations start is fine, but use player skill (or lack thereof) to decide how far the adventurers can improve a monster’s initial reaction. This is not a matter of “my character ought to be really persuasive” — this is one of the places where the player’s skill, not the character’s, is tested.

CHARISMA — Roll 3d6 for your character’s Charisma score. Highly charismatic characters have a better chance to talk their way out of trouble, and can lead more followers than characters with low Charisma. Charisma also limits the number of “special hirelings” your character can attract into service as vassals. This does not mean normal men-at-arms; it means henchmen who are character-types: Magic-Users, Clerics and others. Once your character reaches higher levels, you will need those minions! Charisma does not affect the number of regular troops, lantern-bearers, and pack-carriers you can employ, although it might affect how loyal they are. Charisma is one of two Prime Attributes for Druid characters. Any character with a Charisma score of 13 or higher receives a +5% bonus to all experience point awards.

The process is expensive, and the creator must have achieved a certain level of magical expertise in order to use the book, but these are priceless repositories of forgotten lore. Usable by: Magic-Users only. — Manual of Intelligence: Reading this tome increases the reader’s Intelligence by 1 point (to a maximum of 18). Usable by: All Classes.

CREATING MAGIC ITEMS — Characters can create magic items using the following guidelines, although this is an area where the Referee has total discretion and is free to substitute different rules. — Potions are created by alchemists (see, Hiring Followers), with the more powerful potions usually requiring the help of a Magic-user. A Magic-user seeking to create potions must employ an alchemist. The additional cost of ingredients and time for creating potions should be roughly the same as creating a scroll for a spell of a power level commensurate with the potion's effect (see below). The Referee may certainly use the availability of cheap ingredients as the motivation for an adventure.
Scrolls may be created by Clerics or Magic-users at a cost of 100 gold pieces per level of the spell, and require a week of work for each spell level. Magically-infused inks brewed by alchemists are the main component of this cost, and these may not be readily available outside large cities. — Other magic items may only be created by Magic-users of 11th level or higher. The cost to create a magic item that can be used repeatedly (including rechargeable items such as wands and staffs) follows the guidelines below. These are merely guidelines: the Referee is free to decide that cost or time can be reduced by the availability of already-magical materials, or if the character seeks to place a relatively trivial enchantment upon an object that would ordinarily require a higher base cost. Similarly, items of great power would cost more than these guidelines indicate. — Enchanting a weapon: Enchanting a +1 weapon (or 10 pieces of ammunition) requires 1000 gold pieces and 4 weeks of time. A +2 enchantment costs an additional 3,000, +3 requires 9,000, and +4 requires 27,000 gold pieces. Each additional point of bonus adds an additional 4 weeks. Thus, the cost of a +4 weapon is 40,000 gold pieces.
